This comes from adding percentages (90+85+80), which is incorrect.
85% is just one stage, not the compound result.

toLevel5.sentence: Energy {{blank:1}} describes how concentrated and available energy is for doing useful work. Electrical and chemical energy are considered high quality, while dispersed {{blank:2}} energy at ambient temperature is the lowest quality.

blanks.correctExplanation: Energy quality refers to how available and concentrated energy is for conversion to useful work.

distractors.explanation: Quantity (total amount) is conserved. Quality degrades — these are different concepts.
Energy density is energy per unit volume/mass. Quality is about availability for work.

blanks.correctExplanation: Dispersed thermal energy at ambient temperature has maximum entropy and cannot spontaneously do work.

distractors.explanation: Kinetic energy is generally high quality as it can easily drive machinery.
Nuclear energy is extremely high quality — concentrated and highly available.

toLevel5.sentence: An incandescent bulb converts approximately {{blank:1}}% of electrical energy to light, while an LED converts approximately {{blank:2}}%, making LEDs significantly more efficient for lighting.

blanks.correctExplanation: Incandescent bulbs are notoriously inefficient, converting only about 5% of electricity to visible light.

distractors.explanation: 40% is the approximate efficiency of LEDs, not incandescent bulbs.
90% would make incandescent bulbs extremely efficient, which they are not.

blanks.correctExplanation: LEDs convert approximately 40-50% of electrical energy to visible light.

distractors.explanation: 5% is the efficiency of incandescent bulbs, not LEDs.
95% would be near-perfect efficiency, which no current lighting technology achieves.

toLevel5.sentence: The formula for efficiency is: efficiency = ({{blank:1}} energy output ÷ total energy {{blank:2}}) × 100%, and the result can never exceed 100% for any real system.

blanks.correctExplanation: Only the useful (desired) output counts in the numerator of the efficiency calculation.

distractors.explanation: Total output equals total input (conservation), which would always give 100%.
Wasted energy goes in neither numerator nor denominator — it is the complement of efficiency.

blanks.correctExplanation: Total energy input is the denominator — what was supplied to the system.

distractors.explanation: Using output in the denominator would give useful/total output, not true efficiency.
Stored energy is a snapshot, not the throughput measured for efficiency.

toLevel5.sentence: Regenerative braking recovers approximately {{blank:1}}% of kinetic energy in an electric vehicle, which is stored as {{blank:2}} energy in the battery for later use.

blanks.correctExplanation: Regenerative braking typically recovers about 60-70% of kinetic energy during deceleration.

distractors.explanation: 100% recovery is impossible due to electrical resistance, mechanical friction and conversion losses.
10% dramatically underestimates modern regenerative braking capability.

blanks.correctExplanation: The recovered electrical energy is stored as chemical energy in the battery's electrochemical cells.

distractors.explanation: The whole point is that kinetic energy is being converted for storage, not kept as kinetic.
If energy were stored as heat, it would dissipate. Batteries store energy chemically.

toLevel5.sentence: A coal power station at 35% efficiency, 95% transmission, powering a motor at 90% efficiency gives an overall source-to-output efficiency of {{blank:1}}%, calculated by {{blank:2}} the three individual efficiencies.

distractors.explanation: This incorrectly averages the three values: (35+95+90)/3.
This adds the percentages: 35+95+90=220, which is meaningless.

blanks.correctExplanation: Overall efficiency = product of individual stage efficiencies.

distractors.explanation: Adding efficiencies is mathematically incorrect for compound systems.
Averaging overestimates the true compound efficiency.

toLevel5.sentence: The well-to-wheel efficiency of a petrol car is approximately {{blank:1}}%, while an electric vehicle powered by renewable electricity achieves approximately {{blank:2}}%, demonstrating the superior efficiency of the electric pathway.

blanks.correctExplanation: Petrol cars achieve about 20-25% overall efficiency from crude oil extraction to wheel motion.

distractors.explanation: 85% is the battery-to-wheel efficiency of an EV, not the well-to-wheel of a petrol car.
50% significantly overestimates petrol car well-to-wheel efficiency.

blanks.correctExplanation: With renewable electricity (no generation losses to count), EVs achieve about 70-80% well-to-wheel efficiency.

distractors.explanation: 20% is the petrol car figure, not the renewable-powered EV figure.
100% is impossible due to battery charging, motor and drivetrain losses.
